text: My cousin. So the prophet calls Christ, as being of his family and kindred, by descending from the house of David. (Challoner) (Menochius)<hr>Hebrew and Septuagint, "beloved." Dod may also mean a near relation. (Calmet)<hr>Isaias being of the same tribe, sets before us the lamentations of Christ over Jerusalem, Luke 19:41. (Worthington)<hr>The Hebrews had canticles of sorrow, as well as of joy. The prophet thus endeavours to impress more deeply on the minds of the people what he had been saying. The master of the vineyard is God himself, ver. 7. (Calmet)<hr>Hill. Literally, in the horn, the son of oil. (Challoner)<hr>The best vines grew among olive and fig trees. (Doubdan 21.)<hr>Septuagint, "in a horn, (mountain) in a fat soil." (Haydock)

Stones. They burn and starve in different seasons, Colossians 12:3.<hr>Choicest. Hebrew sorek. (Haydock)<hr>There was a famous valley of this name, Judges 16:4. The angels guarded the vineyard, in which Abraham, Moses, etc., were found.<hr>Tower. To keep the wine, etc., Matthew 21:33. It denotes the temple, (Calmet) Scriptures, etc. (Menochius)<hr>Wild. Sour, Deuteronomy 32:32.
Judge. God condescends to have his conduct scrutinized, Isaias 41:1.
Was it. "Why has it produced wild grapes, while I looked?" etc.
Down. By the Chaldeans, and after the death of Christ. (Calmet)<hr>When God withdraws his aid, man is unable to stand. Yet he falls by his own fault, which God only permits. (Worthington)
It. During the whole of the captivity, the land might keep its sabbaths, Leviticus 26:34. (Calmet)<hr>The people shall be deprived of saving doctrine. (Menochius)

Israel. This comparison is very common, Psalm 79:9., and Matthew 20:1. (Calmet)<hr>The preceding parable is explained. (Menochius)<hr>Cry. For vengeance, Jeremias 12:8., and Genesis 4:10., and 18:20. (Calmet)
Even. Septuagint, "to take from your neighbour: shall," etc. (Haydock)
Things. Unjust practices.<hr>Inhabitant. What will your avarice avail, (Haydock) since you must abandon all? (Calmet)
Measure. Hebrew, "both."<hr>Thirty. Hebrew, "a chomer shall yield an epha."

To follow. Hebrew, "for shecar," (Calmet) palm wine, (Theodoret) or any inebriating liquor. (St. Jerome in Isaias 28.) Our version is conformable to Aquila and Symmachus. (Haydock)<hr>Numbers 6:3., and Ecclesiastes 10:16.
Work. Chastisement, ver. 19., and Isaias 28:21. (Calmet)<hr>They are admonished to observe the festivals of the Lord, and not to indulge in riotousness. (Worthington)
Hell. Or the grave, which never says enough, Proverbs 30:15. Isaias alludes to what should happen under Nabuchodonosor, as if it were past. (G.[Calmet?])
Justice. All will be taught to adore him. (Haydock)
Strangers. Ammonites, etc., (Calmet) shall occupy part of the land. (Haydock)
Cart. Fatiguing themselves with iniquity, (Wisdom 5:7.; Calmet) and delaying your conversion. (St. Isidore) (Menochius)
It. The Jews were often guilty of the like insolence, Jeremias 17:15.
Conceits. Blind guides, Matthew 15:14.

Drink. Hebrew, "mix shecar." People generally mixed wine and water. They also strove who could drink most, and the Greeks had a feast for this purpose, (Calmet) which they styled Choas, for the measure which was to be swallowed down. (Aristophanes, Acharn. act. 4:4. and 5. ultra)<hr>Cyrus the younger boasted to the Greek ambassadors, that "he could drink and bear more wine than his brother." (Plut.[Plutarch?] in Artax.)
Justice. Declaring the righteous guilty, ver. 20. (Haydock)
Still. After the ruin of Jerusalem, the people were led away. (Calmet)<hr>Grievous sins must be severely punished, as was that of the murderers of Christ. (Worthington)

Off. Like a king, leading all his subjects to battle. (Calmet)<hr>Whistle. He alludes to the custom of leading forth bees by music, Isaias 7:18. (St. Cyprian)<hr>Earth. The Chaldeans, (chap. 41:9., and Jeremias 6:22.) and not the Romans, as some would suppose.<hr>Swiftly. Like an eagle, Daniel 7:4., and Jeremias 48:40.
Broken. They shall march incessantly, Ezechiel 26:7., and 30:11.
Hoofs. They were hardened, but not shod. (Xenophon) (Amos 6:13.)
Lion. Nabuchodonosor is compared to one, ver. 26., and Jeremias 4:7.
Mist. Denoting calamity. Hebrew, "ruin." Septuagint, "indigence." (Calmet)
